questions.prompt: How does Tailwind decide which utilities ship in production CSS?

choices.text: JIT compiler scans the files configured in `content` for class string literals; generates CSS only for classes found. No utilities you didn't use ever exist. Scan uses regex on source files — doesn't evaluate JS, doesn't see dynamic strings. Implication: `bg-${color}-500` won't generate (use full strings or safelist). Final CSS is typically 10-100KB even for large apps because only used utilities ship; compare to 200-500KB+ for hand-written legacy CSS.

questions.explanation: JIT scans content paths for class strings; generates only what's found.

choices.text: GPU-accelerated. Transform and opacity don't trigger layout or paint — animations stay 60fps. Width/height/top/left changes trigger layout recalculation on every frame, slow and janky for complex pages. For hover effects, `hover:translate-y-1 hover:scale-105` (transforms) over `hover:py-4` (changes padding). Same for entrance animations: translateY into place, not growing height. Lighthouse flags layout-triggering animations as 'force layout' issues.

questions.explanation: Transform + opacity = GPU-accelerated. Layout properties = janky.

questions.prompt: Modal needs exit animation. Tailwind transitions or Framer Motion?

choices.text: Framer Motion (motion.dev). Tailwind transitions only handle IN smoothly — when an element is unmounted, no element exists to transition. Framer's `AnimatePresence` keeps the element mounted until its exit animation completes, then removes. Pattern: `<AnimatePresence>{show && <motion.div initial exit animate>` — the exit prop fires when show becomes false. Combine Tailwind for static styling + Framer for dynamic motion. Or shadcn's tailwindcss-animate plugin for simpler animate-in/out cases that work via class swaps.

questions.explanation: Framer Motion's AnimatePresence for exit animations. Tailwind transitions only handle IN.

questions.prompt: CSS bundle 250KB; hundreds of arbitrary values (`p-[13px]`, `bg-[#FF5733]`). Cause?

choices.text: Design system drift — the scale and palette don't cover what designs actually need, so devs reach for arbitrary values constantly. Each unique arbitrary value generates a class, bloating output. Fix: add recurring arbitrary values to tailwind.config (`13` to spacing, `'wine': '#FF5733'` to colors) so they consolidate into reused scale steps. Also audit the underlying design choices — usually drift from the system; conversation with design strengthens consistency. Bundle shrinks naturally as scale steps replace one-off arbitrary values.

questions.explanation: Lots of arbitrary values = design system gap. Add to config; audit drift; consolidate.

questions.prompt: Migrating CSS Modules codebase to Tailwind. Best approach?

choices.text: Incremental migration over months. Install alongside existing CSS; disable Tailwind's preflight if it conflicts with legacy reset; write all NEW components in Tailwind from day one; convert existing components opportunistically when feature work touches them. Both systems coexist for months without problems. After 6-12 months, most code is Tailwind; a focused sprint cleans up the rest. Big-bang rewrites almost always fail — too risky, too long, too much regression area. Pragmatism over purity during migration; set a goal of 'N components per sprint' and progress accumulates.

questions.explanation: Incremental migration: new code → Tailwind, existing → convert when touched. Coexist for months.
